revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:祇園造]]
[[File:Yasaka Main Hall 01.jpg|thumb|250px|{{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}} Main Hall]] '''Gion-zukuri''' (祇園造, ぎおんづくり) is one of the styles of Shinto shrine architecture in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is also known as '''Yasaka-zukuri''' (八坂造, やさかづくり).
== Overview == The Gion style found uniquely at {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}} in Kyoto's Gion district features a main hall and a worship hall (currently serving as the haiden) that were originally built as separate structures, all covered by a single roof. This style is considered to be the closest in design to Buddhist temple architecture among Shinto shrine styles.
== Structure == The structure of the Gion style is based on the {{ill|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|en|East Asian hip-and-gable roof|qid=Q855025|lt=Irimoya-zukuri|1=East Asian hip-and-gable roof}} and {{ill|Hirairi|en|Hirairi|qid=Q3136194|lt=Hirairi-zukuri|1=Hirairi}} designs. The main hall is configured with five bays at the front and two bays along the sides, surrounded by eaves ({{ill|Eaves|en|Eaves|qid=Q163972|lt=Hibi|1=Eaves}}), while a worship hall is added at the front. These are all covered by a single roof made with {{ill|Cypress Bark|ja|檜皮葺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cypress Bark|1=cypress bark roof|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q90373477}}. Additionally, the front features a {{ill|Mukaibai|ja|向拝|1=kōhai|2=ja|3=向拝|4=zh|5=向拜|lt=kōhai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mukaibai|13=User:Immanuelle/kōhai (architecture)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11415970}} and the other three sides are adorned with secondary eaves (孫庇), resulting in a large and complex structure.
== History == * In the 5th year of {{ill|Chengping|en|Chengping|qid=Q11076356|lt=Shōhei|1=Chengping}} ({{ill|935|en|935|qid=Q24867|lt=935 AD|1=935}}), a Taiseikan document recorded that the main hall and the worship hall of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}} were separate buildings. * An old drawing from the 3rd year of {{ill|Gentoku|en|Gentoku|qid=Q1327385|lt=Gentoku|1=Gentoku}} ({{ill|1331|en|1331|qid=Q6000|lt=1331 AD|1=1331}}) already depicts the Gion style; however, since the original of that drawing dates to the 2nd year of {{ill|Kanna (era)|en|Kanna (era)|qid=Q1327053|lt=Kanwa|1=Kanna (era)}} ({{ill|986|en|986|qid=Q23785|lt=986 AD|1=986}}), it is estimated that the Gion style was established between 935 and 986 AD. * The current main hall was rebuilt in the 3rd year of {{ill|Jōō (Edo period)|en|Jōō (Edo period)|qid=Q1145081|lt=Shōō|1=Jōō (Edo period)}} ({{ill|1654|en|1654|qid=Q6970|lt=1654 AD|1=1654}}).
== Reference Materials == * 『{{ill|Nijūni-sha Chūshiki|ja|二十二社註式|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijūni-sha Chūshiki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}』 * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}} “Paper-based Colored Gion Shrine Illustration” (an Important Cultural Property)
